content.agent-assisted-coding:
Agent-Assisted Coding (Spec-Driven Development)

The way we write software is changing. Agent-assisted coding shifts the developer's role from writing every line of code to authoring precise specifications that AI agents execute against.

The Conductor Methodology

  • Spec-First: Every feature starts with a spec.md that defines functional requirements, acceptance criteria, and out-of-scope boundaries
  • Plan-Driven: An implementation plan (plan.md) breaks the spec down into discrete, testable tasks with clear commit patterns
  • Test-Driven: Each task follows Red-Green-Refactor — write failing tests first, then implement to pass
  • Checkpointed: Phase boundaries create checkpoint commits with attached verification reports (git notes)

Best Practices

  1. Be Explicit: Ambiguity in a spec produces incorrect code. Define edge cases, error states, and exact output formats
  2. Keep Tasks Small: Each task should be independently testable and committable — if it feels too big, split it
  3. Review Generated Code: AI-generated code must be reviewed for correctness, security, and maintainability — treat it like a PR from a junior developer
  4. Maintain the Loop: Spec → Implement → Review → Archive. Close the loop completely before starting the next feature

Workflow Integration

Spec.md ──→ Plan.md ──→ Implement ──→ Review ──→ Archive/Delete
                 │
           ┌─────┴─────┐
      Test-Driven   Checkpoint
      (Red/Green)   (Git Notes)

When to Use Agent-Assisted Coding

  • Boilerplate code — CRUD endpoints, data models, form validation
  • Well-defined algorithms — sorting, validation, transformation pipelines
  • Test generation — schema validation tests, edge case coverage
  • Documentation — README updates, API docs, changelogs

Limitations

  • Novel architecture decisions require human judgment
  • Security-sensitive code needs manual review
  • Cross-cutting concerns (auth, logging, monitoring) benefit from architectural oversight
  • Ambiguous or incomplete specs produce poor results — garbage in, garbage out
content.ci-cd-pipeline:
CI/CD Pipeline

Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) automates the process of testing, building, and deploying applications. This guide walks through setting up a robust pipeline using GitHub Actions.

Pipeline Stages

A typical CI/CD pipeline consists of:

  1. Source: Triggered by code push or pull request
  2. Build: Compile code and create artifacts
  3. Test: Run unit, integration, and linting checks
  4. Deploy: Push artifacts to staging/production

GitHub Actions Example

name: CI/CD Pipeline
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test

  deploy:
    needs: test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to production
        run: echo "Deploying..."

Best Practices

  • Fast Feedback: Keep build times under 10 minutes
  • Idempotent Builds: Same commit → same artifact every time
  • Secret Management: Use repository secrets, never hardcode credentials
  • Environment Parity: Dev, staging, and production environments should match
  • Rollback Strategy: Always have a plan to revert a deployment
content.database-design-patterns:
Database Design Patterns

Choosing the right database architecture is one of the most consequential decisions in software engineering. This guide covers the fundamental patterns and trade-offs.

SQL vs NoSQL

Dimension SQL (PostgreSQL, MySQL) NoSQL (MongoDB, DynamoDB)
Schema Rigid, predefined Flexible, dynamic
Relations First-class (JOINs, FK) Denormalized, application-managed
Consistency Strong (ACID) Eventual (BASE)
Scaling Vertical (read replicas) Horizontal (sharding)
Use Case Transactions, reporting, ERP Real-time feeds, catalogs, IoT

Normalization Forms

  • 1NF: Each cell contains a single value, no repeating groups
  • 2NF: 1NF + every non-key column depends on the entire primary key
  • 3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependencies (non-key column depends on another non-key column)
  • BCNF: 3NF + every determinant is a candidate key

In practice, most systems stop at 3NF and selectively denormalize for performance.

Indexing Strategies

  • B-Tree: Default — good for equality and range queries. Use on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY
  • Hash Index: Exact-match lookups only — faster than B-Tree for key-value access
  • GiST/GIN: Full-text search, array containment, geospatial queries (PostgreSQL)
  • Covering Index: Include all queried columns to avoid table heap lookups
  • Partial Index: Index only a subset of rows (WHERE status = 'active') — saves space

Sharding Patterns

  • Key-Based (Hash): Distribute rows by hash of a shard key — even distribution, but range queries cross shards
  • Range-Based: Partition by value range (e.g., date ranges) — good for time-series data
  • Directory-Based: A lookup service maps keys to shards — flexible but introduces a single point of failure
  • Consistent Hashing: Minimizes rebalancing when shards are added or removed — popular in distributed caches

Real-World Decision Framework

  1. Data Relationships — are they complex with many-to-many? Use SQL
  2. Access Patterns — is it read-heavy, write-heavy, or mixed?
  3. Consistency Requirements — do you need immediate consistency or is eventual consistency acceptable?
  4. Scale Projections — will you need to scale horizontally in the near future?
  5. Team Expertise — what databases does your team already know well?

The most pragmatic answer is often a polyglot persistence approach: use the right database for each workload within the same system.
content.docker-basics:
Docker Basics

Docker is a platform for developing, shipping, and running applications inside lightweight, portable containers. This article covers the basics you need to get started.

What are Containers?

Containers are lightweight, standalone packages that include everything needed to run a piece of software — code, runtime, system tools, libraries, and settings. Unlike virtual machines, containers share the host operating system kernel.

Key Concepts

  • Images: Read-only templates used to create containers
  • Containers: Runnable instances of images
  • Dockerfile: A text file with instructions to build an image
  • Registry: A repository for storing and sharing images (e.g., Docker Hub)
  • Volumes: Persistent storage for container data

Basic Commands

docker pull nginx          # Download an image
docker build -t my-app .   # Build an image from a Dockerfile
docker run -d -p 80:80 nginx  # Run a container
docker ps                   # List running containers
docker stop <container_id>  # Stop a container
docker logs <container_id>  # View container logs
content.linux-essentials:
Linux Essentials

Linux proficiency is a core skill for any DevOps engineer. This guide covers the essential commands and concepts you need to navigate and manage Linux systems effectively.

File System Navigation

ls -la           # List files with details
cd /path/to/dir  # Change directory
pwd              # Print working directory
find / -name "*.log"  # Search for files
du -sh *         # Check directory sizes

Process Management

ps aux           # List all running processes
top              # Real-time process monitoring
kill -9 <PID>    # Force kill a process
systemctl start <service>  # Manage systemd services
journalctl -u <service>    # View service logs

Permissions

chmod 755 file   # Set file permissions
chown user:group file  # Change file owner
umask            # View/set default permissions

Networking

ss -tulpn        # List listening ports
curl -v http://example.com  # HTTP requests
ping <host>      # Test connectivity
traceroute <host>  # Trace network path
content.llm-fine-tuning:
LLM Fine-Tuning Guide

Fine-tuning adapts a pre-trained large language model to perform specific tasks or understand domain-specific knowledge. This guide covers the end-to-end process from data preparation to deployment.

When to Fine-Tune

Consider fine-tuning when:

  • Domain Specialization: You need the model to understand technical jargon (legal, medical, code)
  • Task Specificity: Standard prompting doesn't achieve the desired output format or quality
  • Data Privacy: You cannot send proprietary data through third-party API endpoints
  • Latency: On-device or self-hosted inference is required

Data Preparation

Raw Data → Clean → Format → Validate → Split (80/10/10)
  • Collect high-quality, task-specific examples (min 500-1000 for noticeable improvements)
  • Format as instruction-response pairs or conversation turns
  • Deduplicate and filter low-quality samples
  • Include edge cases and failure scenarios

Fine-Tuning Techniques

Full Fine-Tuning

  • Updates all model parameters
  • Requires significant compute (multiple GPUs)
  • Best for major domain adaptation

LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)

  • Adds trainable rank-decomposition matrices to attention layers
  • Reduces trainable parameters by 10,000x
  • Memory efficient — fine-tune on a single consumer GPU
  • Easy to swap adapters without full model reload

QLoRA

  • Quantizes the base model to 4-bit precision
  • Adds LoRA adapters on top
  • Fine-tune 65B parameter models on a single 48GB GPU
  • Negligible quality loss compared to full fine-tuning

Hyperparameters

Parameter Typical Range Notes
Learning Rate 1e-5 to 5e-4 Lower for full fine-tuning
Batch Size 4-64 Larger batches with gradient accum
Epochs 1-5 Stop when eval loss plateaus
LoRA Rank (r) 8-64 Higher = more capacity
LoRA Alpha 16-128 Usually 2x the rank
Warmup Steps 50-200 Stabilizes initial training

Evaluation

  • Perplexity: Measure of prediction quality on held-out data
  • Human Evaluation: Rate outputs for relevance, accuracy, and tone
  • Task-Specific Metrics: BLEU for translation, ROUGE for summarization, F1 for classification
  • A/B Testing: Compare fine-tuned vs. base model on real user queries

Deployment

  • Export in GGUF format for CPU-friendly inference (llama.cpp)
  • Use vLLM or TGI for high-throughput GPU serving
  • Dockerize with model weights and inference server
  • Implement a feedback loop to collect real-world performance data
content.microservices-patterns:
Microservices Patterns

Microservices architecture decomposes an application into independently deployable services that communicate over a network. This guide covers the essential patterns you need to build robust microservice-based systems.

Decomposition Patterns

  • Decompose by Business Capability: Align services with business functions (inventory, billing, shipping)
  • Decompose by Subdomain: Use Domain-Driven Design bounded contexts to define service boundaries
  • Strangler Fig: Incrementally replace monolithic components with microservices

Communication Patterns

Service A ──HTTP/REST──→ Service B
    │                        │
    │                        │
    └────Message Queue───────┘
  • Synchronous: REST, gRPC for request-response interactions
  • Asynchronous: Message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ) for event-driven communication
  • API Gateway: Single entry point for routing, auth, rate limiting

Data Management

  • Database per Service: Each service owns its data store — prevents tight coupling
  • Saga Pattern: Distributed transactions via choreography or orchestration
  • CQRS: Separate read and write models for query/command optimization
  • Event Sourcing: Persist state changes as an append-only event log

Observability

  • Distributed Tracing: Trace requests across service boundaries (Jaeger, Zipkin)
  • Centralized Logging: Aggregate logs from all services (ELK stack)
  • Health Checks: /health and /ready endpoints for Kubernetes probes

Deployment

  • Containerization: Docker packages each service with its dependencies
  • Orchestration: Kubernetes manages service deployment, scaling, and healing
  • Service Mesh: Istio/Linkerd handle service-to-service communication, retries, and circuit breaking

Best Practices

  • Design for failure — use circuit breakers, retries, and timeouts
  • Implement CI/CD pipelines per service for independent deployments
  • Version APIs explicitly (e.g., /v1/orders, /v2/orders)
  • Use infrastructure as code for reproducible environments
content.tdd:
Test-Driven Development (TDD)

Test-Driven Development is a disciplined software development practice where you write tests before production code. It's not just about testing — it's about driving the design of your code through its testability.

The Red-Green-Refactor Cycle

Red ──→ Write a failing test that defines the expected behavior
 │
Green ──→ Write the minimum code to make the test pass
 │
Refactor ──→ Clean up the code while keeping tests green

Why TDD Works

  • Design Feedback: Writing the test first forces you to think about the API before the implementation
  • Regression Safety: A comprehensive test suite catches unintended side effects immediately
  • Documentation: Tests serve as executable documentation — they show exactly how the code is supposed to behave
  • Confidence: Refactoring with a good test suite is fearless — if tests pass, the behavior is preserved

Coverage Thresholds

Metric Minimum Target
Lines > 80%
Branches > 75%
Functions > 80%
Statements > 80%

Coverage is a floor, not a ceiling — don't chase arbitrary numbers at the expense of meaningful tests.

TDD in the Conductor Framework

The Conductor methodology enforces TDD at every task level:

  1. Select Task from plan.md
  2. Write Failing Test (Red) — this test defines the acceptance criteria
  3. Implement (Green) — write the minimum code to pass
  4. Verify Coverage — ensure the new code meets project thresholds
  5. Commit with Git Note — attach a task summary to the commit

Common Pitfalls

  • Testing Implementation Details: Test behavior, not internal state. Your tests should survive a complete internal rewrite
  • Brittle Tests: Avoid coupling tests to UI markup strings, file paths, or other volatile details
  • Over-Mocking: Mock external boundaries (network, filesystem), not internal collaborators
  • The Iceberg: Don't commit untested code with the intent to "come back later" — it rarely happens
